glossary.definition: manifestations of intelligent behavior including learning, memory, perception of multiple aspects simultaneously, 'sense of place', and awareness of the neighborhood in plants; also refers to abilities demonstrated by humans and animals such as problem-solving, language use, abstraction formation.
glossary.sentence: they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood.

glossary.definition: a concept encompassing diverse manifestations across humans, animals, and plants. It involves abilities to learn, adapt, make decisions based on environmental information and internal state, with no universally accepted definition due to ongoing scientific debates about whether it is a single ability or multiple abilities.
glossary.sentence: It is problematic to measure intelligence due to the diversity of people... what human intelligence should be adopted as a standard in the development of intelligent computer systems: average intelligence for humanity, the intelligence of some genius individual, or the intelligence of a child with autism spectrum disorder?

glossary.definition: studied across multiple scientific fields including psychology which has developed theories and tests to assess it. Debates exist about whether it is one ability or many, and challenges arise in measurement due to diversity among individuals influenced by linguistic, cultural, physical conditions.
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science... there still no a unified viewpoint if intelligence is a single ability or a set of abilities (Sternberg, 2019; Legg and Hutter, 2007).

glossary.definition: related to learning abilities in animals like monkeys, dolphins, elephants, rats, or crows. Measurement is more challenging than human intelligence due to greater diversity in cognitive capacities, sensory organs, and environmental exploration methods.
glossary.sentence: It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys... it may not be so.

glossary.definition: not based on a brain or nervous system but includes intellectual performances like learning through repeated practice, memory, simultaneous perception of multiple aspects (shape, color, smell, sound), 'sense of place', and neighborhood awareness. Plants gather and update environmental information to make decisions aligning their well-being with their environment.
glossary.sentence: plants possess many forms of intellectual performance... makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment

glossary.definition: a field defined by McCarthy in 1956 as the study to simulate every aspect of learning or other features of intelligence. It is historically connected with human intelligence and aims to develop systems that support people in performing functions through simulation of intelligent behaviors.
glossary.sentence: The first definition of artificial intelligence was offered by John McCarthy... improve themselves...

glossary.definition: an approach used to develop intelligent computer systems where human beings are viewed as information processing systems. Research focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in both the human mind and computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly... stored in the human mind and in computers.

glossary.definition: a method for determining if a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from that of a human. It is used as an approach to develop systems that act humanly by simulating human-like responses.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)

glossary.definition: defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved, formulated as 'A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action'
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: a system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality
glossary.sentence: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: consist of symbols connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) allow the use of existing symbolic structures and creation of new structures
glossary.sentence: Symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures

glossary.definition: allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures in a physical symbol system; operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures modeled after the brain, used for performing tasks like classification and pattern recognition through interconnected artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: a famous example of criticism against physical symbol system hypothesis; describes a situation where a physical system correctly manipulates symbols but has no intelligence
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: a field in which the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies practical and theoretical research; uses symbolic representations for problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems...

glossary.definition: a field in which the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies practical and theoretical research; uses symbolic representations for problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems...

glossary.definition: a field in which the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies practical and theoretical research; uses symbolic representations for problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: a concept in connectionist artificial intelligence where knowledge is considered as a distributed quantity in connected group of smaller processing units rather than encoded in physical symbols
glossary.sentence: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: Pattern recognition assumes identifying structures or patterns in data using artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): ... pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: an example program demonstrating complementary use of connectionist and symbolic paradigms; uses tree-based search complementing artificial neural networks for playing board game Go
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, and is concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: An approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) and is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: An approach that focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.sentence: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration...

glossary.definition: AI techniques that depend on extensive domain-specific knowledge to solve problems effectively, often used in expert systems.
glossary.sentence: strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information
glossary.definition: a category of games characterized by two players competing against each other, alternating moves until the end of the game, complete information about the course of the game for both players, absence of randomness factors, and one player's loss being equal to another player's gain.
glossary.sentence: This topic will explain to you how we can use a state space graph to represent games and thus to create an intelligent computer system that is able to play games against a human or against another intelligent computer system. Taking into account that games are very different in our world, in the context of this study course we will focus on a quite simple type of games – the so-called zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of certain games where one player's loss is equal to another player's gain, resulting in a total sum of zero.
glossary.sentence: one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of games where randomness factors are not involved; outcomes are determined solely by the players' decisions and actions.
glossary.sentence: randomness factors (e. g., the use of a die) are not involved in these types of games.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of games involving exactly two participants competing against each other.
glossary.sentence: two players participate in the game and compete against each other. In the context of our course, these could be a human and an intelligent computer system, or two intelligent computer systems playing against each other;

glossary.definition: a characteristic of games where both players have access to complete information about the course of the game, including past moves, future moves, and available objects used in the game.
glossary.sentence: both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects (e. g. cards or figures) used in the game). Hence, no information is hidden from either player;

glossary.definition: an application of a state space graph specifically tailored for representing games, enabling intelligent computer systems to play against humans or other intelligent systems.
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.2 - A Game Tree - an Application of a State Space Graph In the previous topic, you learnt out that a problem-solving process should be represented in a formal way to ensure a capability of an intelligent computer system to solve problems. A state space graph could be used for this purpose.

glossary.definition: standards or methods used to organize different types of games into categories based on specific characteristics such as the number of players, availability of information, and level of randomness.
glossary.sentence: These classification criteria can be combined to create narrower categories of games, for example (Figure 1): Deterministic two-player games of perfect information...

glossary.term: deterministic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information are those where moves are made alternately, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information, node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.term: stochastic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: games where both players have complete information about the course of the game but randomness is introduced through elements like dice, making precise predictions impossible.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.term: stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information
glossary.definition: games involving multiple players where part of the game's information is hidden from participants and randomness may be introduced.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: Intermediate states are produced when each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process

glossary.definition: Goal states are those in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4).
glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states, in which three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: Moves are actions by players making by putting their marks in empty squares.
glossary.sentence: To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information is defined as one where an arc drawn between two nodes at the same level would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which contradicts its principle.
glossary.sentence: If an arc was drawn between two nodes at the same level, this would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: Parameters are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different.
glossary.sentence: These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different; for example, they may include the score of each player, the current game board, or both the scores of the players and the current game board, etc.

glossary.term: complexity of a state space graph

glossary.sentence: All or most of the data are given in the initial problem statement. For example, the interpretation of an Rtg image of an organ of the human body fits this search direction because all the data (pixels of the image, their colour and density) are given and the goal is to find areas indicating a pathology (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph by backtracking to the most recent node with unexamined descendants/ancestors when the current path fails to reach the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms... if the search goal node is not reached by the current path, then it is necessary to backtrack to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and to try another path.

glossary.definition: a situation in a state space graph where following paths leads back to previously visited nodes, potentially causing the search algorithm to get stuck in an infinite loop.
glossary.sentence: the node H is connected to the node D, which is connected to the node C, together making a cycle. In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that perform operational steps such as selecting nodes, checking if they are the goal node, and expanding them without using heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that differ from uninformed search algorithms by requiring an additional input of a heuristic evaluation function and having operational steps to evaluate nodes using this function after expansion.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2). Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function. Moreover, heuristic search algorithms have two additional steps...

glossary.definition: The OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding iteration 0 and cases when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width. The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the search goal node are reached.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width. The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.

glossary.definition: The CLOSED list contains nodes that have been expanded by the algorithm. Nodes are moved to the CLOSED list after being evaluated and selected based on heuristic measures, excluding those not chosen as best nodes.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list... The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The node D is also moved to the CLOSED list as a generated node...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs exhaustive search of the state space graph by going deeper into the graph and expanding nodes until a goal node is found or no further expansion is possible. It guarantees finding the search goal if it exists, but does not guarantee finding the shortest path.
glossary.sentence: Depth-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if it exists in the state space graph because this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the state space graph. However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node...

glossary.definition: a data structure used in algorithms like Depth-First Search where new elements are added to the left side (Last in, First Out principle).
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.

glossary.definition: A single cycle of operations performed by an algorithm during search. In Bidirectional Search, each iteration involves expanding current nodes from both directions and checking for a common node between the two OPEN lists.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node - the node A - is added to the OPEN list of data-driven search. Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the node A and the node K simultaneously.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores a state space graph by deepening but with a set depth limit, preventing indefinite exploration along deep paths; however, it cannot find solutions beyond this depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: A predefined value set before algorithm execution, which restricts how deep the algorithm can explore within the state space graph. Nodes at this level are treated as leaf/root nodes and no further exploration is conducted below them.
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation. ... The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node. Let us consider an example of Depth-Limited Search by implementing data-driven search in the state space graph you see in Figure 13. The depth limit is 2.

glossary.definition: A numerical value assigned to each node indicating its position relative to the starting node, calculated as an increment from the current node’s depth level. The search start node has a depth level of 0.
glossary.sentence: The depth level of the search start node is equal to 0. The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.

glossary.definition: Nodes located at the defined depth limit in Depth-Limited Search, which are treated as terminal points and not expanded further due to reaching the exploration boundary set by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction). Therefore, the algorithm does not consider nodes located below the defined depth limit.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores closer branches first and then proceeds to the ends of branches in a state space graph. It is associated with high consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is considered its main problem.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is the main problem with Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: A data structure used by Breadth-First Search where new nodes are added to the right side, following the 'First in, First Out' principle.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: Descendants are nodes that result from expanding a given node. They represent possible next states or paths in the search space.
glossary.sentence: ...expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E...

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions. It uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists, which are filled in simultaneously in both directions. The algorithm checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists in each iteration. It stops when a common node is found or the state space is fully searched without finding a common node.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.

glossary.definition: A type of search algorithm that uses heuristic knowledge (experience-based rules) to improve the effectiveness of finding a search goal node by distinguishing between paths that lead to the goal and those that do not. It is contrasted with uninformed search algorithms, which cannot make such distinctions.
glossary.sentence: The effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic search algorithms. Heuristic knowledge provides rules for choosing those paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Knowledge based on experience that helps solve problems, especially in situations with incomplete information or when exact solutions are not feasible.
glossary.sentence: Humans also have a lot of heuristic knowledge related to routine (daily) situations.

glossary.definition: Resources such as search time and computer memory consumed by algorithms during their operation. Bidirectional Search consumes less of these resources compared to Breadth-First Search, but it has limited usage due to practical constraints like the need to know the exact goal node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is the main problem with Breadth-First Search. Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph representing the target or destination state that an algorithm aims to reach. In Bidirectional Search, it is one of the two starting points (along with the search start node) from which searches are conducted simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Let us consider an example of Bidirectional Search by implementing search on the state space graph that you see in Figure 23, taking into account that the search start node is A and the search goal node is K.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph representing the initial or starting state from which an algorithm begins its search. In Bidirectional Search, it is one of the two starting points (along with the goal node) from which searches are conducted simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Bidirectional Search by implementing search on the state space graph that you see in Figure 23, taking into account that the search start node is A and the search goal node is K.

glossary.definition: A node shared between two OPEN lists in a Bidirectional Search algorithm. The presence of such a node indicates that the searches from the start node and the goal node have intersected, allowing the algorithm to stop its operation.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, after expanding the current nodes, it checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists. The node E is in both lists; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: Ancestors are nodes that precede a given node in the search tree. They represent previous states or paths leading to the current node.
glossary.sentence: ...a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width...

glossary.definition: A modern equivalent of heuristic knowledge in human life, referring to experience-based strategies used to solve problems or accomplish tasks more effectively and efficiently.
glossary.sentence: The widely used concept of “life hack” corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge. Taking into account that a problem can have a large number of solutions, people use strategies (heuristic methods) to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way (Eysenck & Keane, 2000).

glossary.definition: A function used in algorithms like Minimax and Alpha-beta pruning to acquire heuristic measures for terminal nodes and propagate them back up to determine the best state for move selection in games.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Min-...valuation function to find the most promising moves. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state...

glossary.definition: Heuristic measures are values assigned by an intelligent computer system to nodes in the available graph. These allow the system to select promising moves during gameplay.
glossary.sentence: ...the intelligent computer system assigns heuristic measures to the nodes of the available graph...

glossary.definition: A component of the heuristic evaluation function representing the distance between the search start node and node n, used by the algorithm to choose shorter available paths.
glossary.sentence: g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.

glossary.definition: A component of the heuristic evaluation function representing a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n, estimating the distance from node n to the search goal node and helping the algorithm choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A representation of the heuristic evaluation function that incorporates multiple problem-specific factors with assigned weights, used to evaluate nodes in practical applications.
glossary.sentence: The heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program.

glossary.definition: A game where an intelligent computer system uses the number of potentially winning positions for the computer as a factor in evaluating nodes and selecting moves to maximize its chances of winning.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: A game with 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares where players move tiles to achieve the goal state, using heuristic evaluation functions based on factors like the number of tiles out of place or summing distances from intended places.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares.

glossary.definition: The branching factor (B) in a state space graph refers to the average number of arcs per node. It is usually calculated by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The depth (L) of a state space graph refers to the length of the longest path within the graph, typically measured as the number of arcs from the initial node to the farthest goal node.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A problem in heuristic evaluation where limited look-ahead may fail to detect promising paths, leading to suboptimal decisions and potential loss in games.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect. When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path...

glossary.definition: Tile reversals refer to a situation in a game where two tiles located close one to another need their places changed, and it usually takes more than two moves to put them back. In our case, all three nodes receive a value of 0 because there are no such situations on the board.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back. In our case, all three nodes will receive a value of 0, because there are no such situations on the board.

glossary.term: number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: The number of tiles out of place is a factor used in heuristic evaluation functions to count how many tiles are not in their correct positions. It can be used alone or combined with other factors such as sum of distances and tile reversals.
glossary.sentence: We have found three different factors for the same game (Figure 9). We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals. We can make combinations of these functions to create more complex heuristic evaluation functions.

glossary.definition: The sum of distances is a factor used in heuristic evaluation functions that calculates the total distance between tiles and their goal positions. The smaller this sum, the closer a state is to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The logic is also very similar here: the smaller the sum of distances, the closer a state is to the search goal node. Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 6, while the second node will get a value of 4, and therefore, this could be considered a promising move.

glossary.definition: A partly available state space graph is when only a part of the entire state space graph can be stored and processed due to limited computational resources. As a result, during system operation, the graph is generated up to a specified depth.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph) . It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources. As a result, during the operation of an intelligent computer system, the state space graph is generated to the specified depth that is allowed by available computational resources.

glossary.definition: A fully available state space graph is when computational resources are sufficient to store and process the entire graph. In such cases, the use of a heuristic evaluation function may not always be necessary.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph) . Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph. For example, in planning problems that must be solved before a robot starts acting in some environment, an action plan is drawn up and represented as a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A property used to reduce the number of states in a game tree by considering equivalent states as one, such as not distinguishing between different corners in Tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12). At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph by selecting nodes with the smallest heuristic measures and expanding them until either the goal is reached or no better node exists among descendants.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. ... The search stops when: the search goal node is reached or when a node whose heuristic measure is better than the measures of its descendants/ancestors is found.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node in the search tree that estimates its potential outcome. It is used by the algorithm to make decisions about which branches to explore further or prune, based on available information and evaluation criteria.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: ...the intermediate heuristic measure of the current node... Step 15: ...acquires a precise heuristic measure... Step 20: When a precise heuristic measure has been assigned... Step 22: ...assigns an intermediate heuristic measure...

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that always chooses a node whose heuristic measure is either higher or lower than the current node's depending on whether the best heuristic measure is defined as the highest or smallest, respectively. It may suffer from local maximum and plateau problems if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: On the one hand, the Hill Climbing algorithm is able to find the search goal node very quickly if the heuristic evaluation function is well-defined. On the other hand, if the function is not well-defined, Hill Climbing has several shortcomings; the most common of these shortcomings are the following (Figure 6): the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction ); the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A situation where Hill Climbing fails to reach the search goal because it encounters a node that has a better heuristic measure than all its descendants or ancestors, preventing further progress toward the goal.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction ).

glossary.definition: A situation where Hill Climbing cannot continue searching because the best descendant or ancestor's heuristic measure is equal to the current node's, leading to no improvement.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration, maintains all previously discovered nodes, and orders them based on their heuristic measures. It avoids local maximum and plateau problems by using a priority queue (OPEN list) to manage nodes.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before. This allows the algorithm to switch between different paths in the state space graph, and it does not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that balances between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search by expanding a limited number of best nodes in each iteration, determined by a beam width parameter set before the search begins. It avoids storing all previously found nodes but still explores multiple paths simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before. This algorithm selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.

glossary.definition: The beam width defines the maximum number of nodes that can be selected at each iteration based on heuristic measures. It determines how many best nodes are added to the OPEN list during expansion.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width...

glossary.definition: The current node is the node being processed at a given iteration. It is selected from the first position in the OPEN list and becomes the focus for expansion.
glossary.sentence: ...the algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node...

glossary.definition: A data structure used in Best-First Search as an OPEN list that processes nodes based on their priority (i.e., heuristic measure), ensuring the best node according to this metric is always processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A complete state space graph includes all states from the initial state of the game (root node) to all goal states (leaf nodes). It represents the entire possible game progression.
glossary.sentence: ...a complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game... A complete state space graph is typically used in simple games...

glossary.definition: A subset of a full state space graph that does not include all possible states or transitions. It is used when it is impractical to evaluate every potential configuration, requiring alternative strategies for decision-making.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: ...these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A player in the game whose objective is to maximise their score, typically represented by nodes at MAX levels where higher heuristic measures are desired.
glossary.sentence: since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score...

glossary.definition: A player in the game whose objective is to minimise the opponent's (maximiser’s) score, typically represented by nodes at MIN levels where lower heuristic measures are desired.
glossary.sentence: since this state the minimiser has the higher score and wins the game...

glossary.definition: Terminal nodes are the end points of a partial state space graph at a specified depth. They represent states that cannot be further expanded within the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: ...the terminal nodes of the sub-graph at the specified depth, even though it is not possible to identify a game result...

glossary.definition: The final outcome determined by evaluating all relevant nodes in the game tree. It represents the best possible score or position achievable for either player based on their respective objectives.
glossary.sentence: Step 25: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: a path in the complete state space graph that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying conditions where all nodes are closely related and have the same heuristic measures equal to the game result; it is not a path that definitely leads to a win by a particular player, but shows the course of the game most likely to be undertaken.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result. In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that applies heuristic measures propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on principles for finding the best state after performing steps in a game.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A principle used alongside Minimax or as an alternative, contributing to the process of selecting moves in a game by applying heuristic evaluation functions and determining best states through propagated measures.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: A definitive value assigned by the algorithm after evaluating all necessary information or reaching a terminal state. It represents an exact evaluation of the node's potential without further uncertainty.
glossary.sentence: Step 15: ...acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far... Step 20: When a precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level... Step 24: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure...

glossary.definition: A value assigned by the algorithm when it cannot determine a precise heuristic measure, indicating that further evaluation is needed. It represents an estimate of the node's potential based on available information.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure... Step 18: ...the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1... Step 22: ...assigns an intermediate heuristic measure of <=−1... Step 23: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1, and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in a game tree where the player's objective is to maximize their outcome. It represents nodes where the maximising player makes decisions based on achieving the best possible result for themselves.
glossary.sentence: Step 17: ...the node is located at the MAX level... Step 21: The current node is located at the MAX level...

glossary.definition: A level in a game tree where the player's objective is to minimize the outcome for the opposing maximiser. It represents nodes where the minimising player makes decisions based on reducing potential gains for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: Step 16: The current node is located at the MIN level... Step 22: ...the node is located at the MIN level...

glossary.definition: A pruning technique performed when the β value of the current node is smaller than the α value of its ancestor, resulting in cutting off all unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: Step 23: ...the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A parent or higher-level node in the search tree relative to a given node. It refers to nodes along the same path from the root that are located above the current node.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: ...checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure... Step 23: When the algorithm has assigned this intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A child or lower-level node in the search tree relative to a given node. It refers to nodes that are directly connected below the current node along its branches.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: ...the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1... Step 15: ...based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far... Step 20: ...acquires the precise heuristic measure of its descendant... Step 25: The descendants of the current node have heuristic measures of 1 and −1.

glossary.definition: A sequence of numbers used in an example game scenario, representing states or positions within the game.
glossary.sentence: Now let’s consider them using the previous example of the game with the numerical string and a score of 8 for each player (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A branch in the game tree that has not yet been evaluated by the algorithm, which may lead to leaf nodes or other unexplored states.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node...

glossary.definition: A lower-level node in the game tree that has not yet been evaluated, influencing whether precise or intermediate heuristic measures can be assigned.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm assigns an intermediate heuristic measure of <=−1... because it is located at MIN level and the only known heuristic measure is a measure of −1.

glossary.definition: A pruning technique performed when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of its ancestor, resulting in cutting off all unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: ...the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: Branches or routes in the search tree that have not yet been evaluated by the algorithm. These represent potential future moves or states that could be explored but are currently excluded from consideration.
glossary.sentence: Step 14: ...cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node... Step 18: ...the algorithm goes down again, via the first unconsidered path... Step 23: ...cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A method that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n in game algorithms, determining predefined depths for analysis based on computational resources.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n. The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: A search strategy used under time constraints to explore partial state space graphs iteratively, increasing depth at each iteration until sufficient analysis or time expiration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead... It starts with depth limit of 1, and evaluates nodes at this depth using a heuristic evaluation function...

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building, enabling systems to learn from data, identify patterns, and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, machine learning is a highly topical area in the field of computer science... Machine learning is a sub-field of artificial intelligence. It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning where algorithms learn from labeled data to perform tasks like regression and classification.
glossary.sentence: It is known that supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task. Previously you have learnt that linear regression is suitable for the regression task. Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours. According to (Atul, 2019), unsupervised learning can be characterised as
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours. glossary.definition: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as

glossary.definition: Algorithms explicitly programmed by developers with precise instructions for each step, providing outputs only to known inputs and typically unable to handle unknown or complex data scenarios effectively.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs...

glossary.definition: a single unit within a dataset that can be grouped into clusters during the hierarchical clustering process.
glossary.sentence: Each data object is initially attributed to its own cluster, and throughout the agglomerative or divisive hierarchical clustering processes, these objects are merged or split based on their dissimilarities.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A parameter whose value is set before the learning process begins and controls aspects of the algorithm's behavior.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of providing examples with real classifications to a perceptron so it can adjust its weights and improve performance. Training involves multiple iterations through datasets.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label... the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if different, adjusts weights...

glossary.definition: the collection of examples used for training a perceptron. The learning process continues until some termination criterion is reached.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron continues the learning process... for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached.

glossary.definition: Classification involves deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;

glossary.definition: a type of supervised learning task where the goal is to predict a continuous output variable based on input features.
glossary.sentence: It is known that supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task. Previously you have learnt that linear regression is suitable for the regression task. Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning task that divides an available dataset into subsets (clusters) sharing similarities, without using labelled data.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering . It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: a type of linear regression with a unique solution because the weights can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010): However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task. There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: a case where calculating weights for linear regression is not trivial and requires more complex methods due to potential issues with matrix computations.
glossary.sentence: However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task. There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity. Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.). Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent.

glossary.definition: a mathematical solution that exists for linear regression with multiple input features but is not used in practice due to issues like matrix singularity and high computational complexity.
glossary.sentence: There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity. Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.). Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent.

glossary.definition: a problem that can occur during matrix computations in linear regression with multiple input features, leading to computational failures.
glossary.sentence: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity. Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.). Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent.

glossary.definition: the level of difficulty or resources required to perform a computation, such as inverting matrices in linear regression with multiple input features.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high (Geron, 2017). Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.). Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent.

glossary.definition: an optimisation technique used in linear regression to find the optimal weights by calculating partial derivatives with respect to all independent variables and minimising the cost function.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector. The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: a mathematical function used in linear regression that measures the error between predicted and actual output values, which is minimised using gradient descent.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Actually, you do not need to know the calculus underlying the gradient descent in detail because the tools, frameworks and languages that exist for using linear regression will automatically handle these computations. However, if you are interested in more information on gradient descent, please, use these sources: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used.

glossary.definition: a positive constant (small real number between 0 and 1) that determines how quickly weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change...

glossary.definition: classification task with two possible classes/categories represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: classification task with more than two classes to which data objects can belong, extending binary classification using averaging techniques across classes like macro or weighted averaging for performance evaluation.
glossary.sentence: The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: a supervised learning algorithm used for binary classification tasks that predicts class labels by applying a sigmoid function to the output of a linear function.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: A sigmoid function is one of the four common choices for determining neuron output along with step, sign, and linear functions.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: a specific value (usually 0.5) in logistic regression that determines whether a predicted probability should be classified as one class or another based on the output of the sigmoid function.
glossary.sentence: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object. If the squashed value is greater than the threshold value, the algorithm assigns a class label of 1 (success, good, yes, win, etc.), else it assigns a class label of 0 (failure, bad, no, loss, etc.)

glossary.definition: a supervised machine learning algorithm used for both regression and classification tasks by finding the nearest data points in the feature space to make predictions.
glossary.sentence: Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks. Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task.

glossary.definition: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the model learns from labeled training data, which includes input-output pairs.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: predicting class/category to which a particular data object belongs with actual class labels given for determining correctness of predicted class labels as part of supervised machine learning. It divides into binary classification (two possible classes) and multiclass classification (more than two classes).
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs. Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels. The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: making predictions in a continuous range with real value output where actual labels are given for determining correctness of predicted labels as part of supervised machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for regression tasks In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value. Since the regression is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.

glossary.definition: A method used to determine the similarity between data objects, often based on distance measurements.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: A numerical value that represents how far apart two data objects are in the feature space.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: Descriptions of data objects based on their features or characteristics.
glossary.sentence: The similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).

glossary.definition: Similarity between data objects based on their features or characteristics.
glossary.sentence: The similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).
