glossary.definition: a method used in the kNN algorithm to calculate the geometric distance between data objects for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: a method used in the kNN algorithm to calculate the geometric distance between data objects for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: a method used in the kNN algorithm to count the number of features in which two categorical data objects differ, indicating their similarity.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: Feature values represented as discrete categories or classes rather than continuous numerical values.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ.

glossary.definition: Feature values that can take any numerical value within a range rather than being limited to discrete categories.
glossary.sentence: Suppose values of all features in the dataset are continuous.

glossary.definition: Data that includes both continuous and categorical feature values within the same dataset.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features.

glossary.definition: A version of the kNN algorithm where the classification is based on finding a single nearest neighbour in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A version of the kNN algorithm where classification is based on considering multiple nearest neighbours defined by the hyperparameter k.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A method used in the kNN algorithm where multiple nearest neighbours vote on the class label for a new data object, with the majority class being selected.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: A property of distance metrics stating that for any three points, the sum of the lengths of any two sides must be greater than or equal to the length of the remaining side.
glossary.sentence: the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality:

glossary.definition: A situation in classification where multiple classes receive the same number of votes from the voting mechanism.
glossary.sentence: If the problem is binary classification (there are only two classes), then k should be an odd number to prevent ties.

glossary.definition: a measure used in the kNN algorithm indicating how often predictions are incorrect, which should be minimised when choosing the hyperparameter k.
glossary.sentence: It is practical to create a plot between error rate and k values, later choosing the k value providing a minimum error rate.

glossary.definition: a hyperparameter in the kNN algorithm representing the chosen number of neighbours examined for making predictions.
glossary.sentence: It is practical to create a plot between error rate and k values, later choosing the k value providing a minimum error rate.

glossary.definition: a parameter in the K-Means algorithm specifying the number of clusters that need to be generated.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: the most frequent class among the selected k neighbours in the classification task of the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class);

glossary.definition: the average value among the selected k neighbours in the regression task of the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: a preprocessing step required by the kNN algorithm to ensure that all features are on a comparable scale before calculating distances.
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value.

glossary.definition: subsets of data created through clustering, where each cluster contains one or more data objects characterised by similarity within the cluster and differences between clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.).

glossary.definition: a measure used to determine how alike data objects are within clusters in unsupervised machine learning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.). Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: a popular unsupervised machine learning algorithm that groups data objects into K clusters based on their distance from centroids, aiming to minimize the sum of distances between data objects and their respective cluster centroids.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” . The algorithm is based on two central concepts: the concept of distance; the concept of a centroid.

glossary.definition: a central point representing a cluster in K-Means clustering, calculated as the average of feature vectors of all data objects within that cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a bottom-up approach in which each data object starts as its own cluster and pairs are recursively merged until one cluster remains.
glossary.sentence: This method involves merging clusters with the smallest distance between them at each step, progressively reducing the number of clusters from N to 1.

glossary.definition: a group of similar data objects formed during the hierarchical clustering process.
glossary.sentence: Clusters are created by merging (in agglomerative) or splitting (in divisive) data objects, with the aim to reduce or increase the number of groups based on intergroup dissimilarity measures.

glossary.definition: a top-down approach that starts with all data objects in a single cluster and recursively splits one cluster into two until each object forms its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: This method involves splitting the most dissimilar elements at each step, increasing the number of clusters from 1 to N.

glossary.definition: a measure used to determine how different or similar two clusters are based on their data objects' distances.
glossary.sentence: In agglomerative clustering, pairs of clusters with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity are merged, while divisive clustering splits those with the largest between-group dissimilarity.

glossary.definition: techniques used to calculate the similarity (distance) between clusters for merging decisions in hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Common linkage methods include Complete-linkage, Single-linkage, and Average-linkage, each defining cluster distance differently based on element distances within clusters.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the similarity between two clusters is determined by calculating the longest distance between any pair of their data objects.
glossary.sentence: This method uses the maximum distance (d) between elements i from cluster G and i' from cluster H to define cluster similarity.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the similarity between two clusters is determined by calculating the shortest distance between any pair of their data objects.
glossary.sentence: This method uses the minimum distance (d) between elements i from cluster G and i' from cluster H to define cluster similarity.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the similarity between two clusters is determined by calculating the average distance between all pairs of their data objects.
glossary.sentence: This method computes an average of distances (d) among elements i from cluster G and i' from cluster H, considering NG and NH as the number of objects in each cluster.

glossary.definition: a tree-like diagram that visually represents hierarchical relationships between data objects resulting from clustering.
glossary.sentence: It provides a graphical format showing how clusters merge or split at different hierarchy levels, with clades indicating merging points and leaves representing individual data objects.

glossary.definition: a branch in the dendrogram that represents the merging of two or more data objects into a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Clades have specific heights corresponding to the dissimilarity at which clusters were merged, aiding in interpreting cluster similarity.

glossary.definition: the terminal point in a dendrogram's clade that corresponds to an individual data object from the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Leaves are used to trace back how each data object was grouped during clustering processes like agglomerative or divisive methods.

glossary.definition: a horizontal line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters by intersecting vertical lines at specific hierarchy levels.
glossary.sentence: The intersection points indicate how many clusters should be formed, with different cut-offs leading to varying numbers of groups based on desired similarity thresholds.

glossary.definition: quantitative measures used to assess the performance of a machine learning algorithm across various tasks such as classification, regression, or clustering.
glossary.sentence: These metrics help compare algorithms and tune hyperparameters by analyzing how well they perform with different numbers of clusters in hierarchical clustering contexts.

glossary.definition: measures average of squared prediction errors for individual data objects providing positive non-zero values with same units as quantity being estimated but in squared units, prone to outliers where smaller value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values. It is calculated in the following way: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent

glossary.definition: applies square root to MSE providing error measure in same unit as target variable where smaller value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: average of sums of absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values providing error measure in same unit as target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: coefficient of determination indicating how close model built fits actual data objects with values ranging from [0,1] where 0 indicates no fit and 1 perfect fit; represents extent observed variance in actual output values is explained by features of constructed model.
glossary.sentence: The R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit. It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model.

glossary.definition: correctly predicted positive class where data object is actually positive and algorithm recognises it as such.
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: correctly predicted negative class where data object is actually negative and algorithm recognises it as such.
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: erroneously predicted positive class where data object is actually negative but algorithm labels it as positive.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.

glossary.definition: erroneously predicted negative class where data object is actually positive but algorithm labels it as negative.
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.

glossary.definition: two-dimensional table summarising outcomes of classification task with rows representing actual classes and columns predicted classes showing counts of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: evaluates fraction of correct predictions over given set of data objects calculated by summing main diagonal values of confusion matrix and dividing by total number of data objects with value range [0,1] where larger value indicates better predictive accuracy but may not be clear indicator if classes are imbalanced.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model. However, this model is not really predictive and Accuracy is not the right performance metric to evaluate the power of this model.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example for the dataset containing 100 data objects, 95 of which labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1. “In this case, a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model. However, this model is not really predictive and Accuracy is not the right performance metric to evaluate the power of this model” (Kubat, 2017) . Figure 1 shows the calculation of accuracy for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: the fraction of correct predictions among all data objects.
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 shows the calculation of accuracy for both binary and multiclass classification tasks. A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes. Figure 2 shows the calculation of precision for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: the fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes. Figure 2 shows the calculation of precision for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: the fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of recall lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives. After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset. Figure 3 shows the calculation of recall for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) . Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. It is calculated in the following way (Chapelle et al., 2006; Geron, 2017) : The value of the F1 score lies in the range [0,1]. A high F-score indicates that the precision and recall are high and close to each other (Chapelle et al., 2006) . Thus, the better the model performance is. In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall. Figure 4 shows the calculation of the F1 score for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.

glossary.definition: the receiver operating characteristic curve that plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) . The ideal case is represented by a situation when the curve reaches the upper-left corner that represents zero error rate on the false positives and 100% accuracy on the true positives (Kubat, 2017) . It can be concluded that the worst-case scenario (random chance) consists of a 45 degrees diagonal line. ROC curve can be calculated for every class in multiclass classification versus all other classes. Figure 5 demonstrates an example of the ROC curve.

glossary.definition: the area under the ROC Curve that provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) . It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017) . AUC can be calculated for each class vs other classes for the multiclass classification task and then averaged across all classes.

glossary.definition: the score calculated by average intra-cluster distance (a - average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (average distance between all clusters). It represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters). Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. The values of the Silhouette score lies in a range [-1, 1]. The closer values of the Silhouette score is to +1, the better is clusters are separated. A value of 0 indicates that the distance between neighbouring clusters is not significant. Hence, data objects lie on the decision boundary. Negative values of the Silhouette score indicate that data objects might have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

glossary.definition: A measure represented on the Silhouette Plot, with cluster label on y-axis and score on x-axis, where size of silhouettes is proportional to number of data objects in a specific cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6). The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: A plot used for visualizing Silhouette Scores, with cluster label on y-axis and score on x-axis, where size of silhouettes reflects the number of data objects in clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6). The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: Tasks in which artificial neural networks are used for classification, involving labeled data and explicit feedback during training process.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks. This topic will look mainly at artificial neural networks, which can be used for the classification task of supervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: Tasks in which artificial neural networks are applied without labeled data, where patterns and structures are discovered from input data without explicit feedback during training process.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks. This topic will look mainly at artificial neural networks, which can be used for the classification task of supervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: A concept closely related to artificial neural networks that is widely recognized, involving multiple layers of processing units to learn hierarchical representations from data.
glossary.sentence: Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks have found application in various fields...

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain with a cell body (soma), dendrites for receiving signals and an axon for transmitting signals through synapses to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions of a biological neuron that receive signals from other neurons, appearing like a little brush.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches...

glossary.definition: A single extension of a biological neuron that transmits electrical impulses to synapses, which then communicate with dendrites or cell bodies of other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has... many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush... The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches...

glossary.definition: The junction where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or cell body of another neuron, facilitating signal transmission through electrical impulses.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: Synapses that promote neuronal firing by having positive weights when calculating synaptic strength based on incoming signals.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: Synapses that prevent neuronal firing by having negative weights when calculating synaptic strength based on incoming signals.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: An artificial neuron is a simple processing unit within an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: a set of simple processing units (artificial neurons );

glossary.definition: values in a neural network that are adjusted during learning to allow correct classification. The perceptron learns these values through weight adjustments.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights... the weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule...

glossary.definition: An additional numerical value typically set to one and used as an offset for the line/hyperplane from origin, allowing flexibility beyond just controlling angles of discriminant.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin. If no bias existed, the line (or the hyperplane) would be restricted to pass through the origin...

glossary.definition: a mathematical function applied to the weighted sum at each processing unit in a neural network. It transforms the summed inputs into an output signal.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum... and then applies the activation function... they are mathematically transformed by applying the summation and activation functions...

glossary.definition: The summation function is used in determining the output of a neuron by combining input signals.
glossary.sentence: We will use the summation function further in this topic.

glossary.definition: A foundational mathematical model defined in 1943 by McCulloch and Pitts, which established fundamental concepts for artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron based on the result from the summation function.
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.definition: The step activation function is a type of hard limit function used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.definition: The sign activation function is a type of hard limit function used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.definition: A linear activation function is one of the four common choices for determining neuron output along with step, sign, and sigmoid functions.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural network An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A processing unit, also called a node, is a single neuron in an artificial neural network that produces output and sends it to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other. You already know that each neuron produces an output.

glossary.definition: A node is another term for a single neuron in an artificial neural network, which processes input and sends outputs to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.

glossary.definition: an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.definition: an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network; it sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network. It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers... an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons.

glossary.definition: a layer in a neural network between input and output layers that consists of computing neurons. It performs feature detection but its outputs cannot be directly observed.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons... Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour...

glossary.definition: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction, with processing units receiving input from upstream units and delivering output to downstream units without any loops.
glossary.sentence: Based on the way of connecting neurons, two types of artificial neural networks can be distinguished (Russell & Norvig, 2010): A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: A recurrent network feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system that may reach stable states or exhibit oscillations/chaotic behavior based on previous inputs.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: the process by which a perceptron adjusts its weights to reduce the difference between actual and desired outputs. It involves checking predicted vs real output, adjusting weights if there's a discrepancy.
glossary.sentence: The learning is based on the small adjustments of weights after each training example... checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if different, adjusts weights...

glossary.definition: A learning rule is an algorithm used to adjust weights in artificial neural networks based on differences between desired and actual outputs.
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: Supervised machine learning involves comparing final outputs from an artificial neural network with desired (actual label) outputs to adjust weights through learning rules.
glossary.sentence: It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.

glossary.definition: Fault tolerance refers to an artificial neural network's ability to maintain operation despite partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between them, as knowledge is distributed across the network.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation,

glossary.definition: Graceful degradation describes how an artificial neural network's performance degrades minimally when parts of it are damaged due to distributed knowledge storage.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation,

glossary.definition: Prediction involves identifying diseases from symptoms or causes from effects using artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): ... prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects;

glossary.definition: Optimization involves finding the best organization of constraints using artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): ... optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: Noise filtering involves separating signal from background and factoring out irrelevant components using artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): ... noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: Non-linear estimation determines values of variables that depend complexly on other more accessible variables using artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: at the same time, artificial neural networks are applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): ... non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: a type of neural network with computing neurons in its output layer. In a single layer perceptron, it has only one processing layer. The perceptron learns weights for classification by adjusting them based on differences between predicted and desired outputs.
glossary.sentence: The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer... The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights...

glossary.definition: neurons that are present in the output layer (and possibly hidden layers) and perform processing, as opposed to input layer which does not contain them.
glossary.sentence: there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons... there is an output layer containing the computing neurons...

glossary.definition: the computational operations performed by computing neurons in a neural network. The input layer does not make processing, but the output layer (and hidden layers) do.
glossary.sentence: there is an input layer that ... does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network...

glossary.definition: a neural network with only one processing layer, where all inputs are connected directly to outputs. The title emphasizes this single-layer structure.
glossary.sentence: The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs.

glossary.definition: a condition that determines when to stop the learning process. This could be reaching a pre-defined maximum permissible error or number of iterations.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron continues the learning process... until some termination criterion is reached... can be stopped if total classification error reaches max permissible error or predefined number of iterations achieved.

glossary.definition: a method used to compute weight corrections in a perceptron. The complete rule for adjustments of weights includes this delta rule application.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule... the complete rule for adjustments of the weights...

glossary.definition: configurable parameters in artificial neural networks, such as number of layers, processing units, combination functions, activation functions, and learning rate.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers... function for combining input signals... activation function to be used. A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters...

glossary.definition: a cycle in which an artificial neural network uses all data objects in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: Another hyperparameter is the number of epochs... An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once...

glossary.definition: the sum of squared differences between actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons across all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs...

glossary.definition: A type of neural network that uses backpropagation algorithm for learning. It consists of multiple layers including input layer, hidden layers and output layer.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights... until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved.

glossary.definition: a type of neural network where signals flow only in the forward direction, from input through hidden layers to output. It can model practically any function with appropriate architecture.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network... has greater processing power and can model practically any function...

glossary.definition: an additional input added to each layer (including hidden and output layers) in a neural network. It is part of the weighted sum calculation.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input... Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input...

glossary.definition: the mathematical operation that combines input signals with their corresponding weights in a neural network. It is part of the processing at each computing neuron.
glossary.sentence: they are mathematically transformed by applying the summation and activation functions... Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum...

glossary.definition: a type of classification problem where the goal is to classify input objects into one of two classes. It requires a single processing unit in the output layer.
glossary.sentence: we want to use the neural network for the binary classification task... a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: An algorithm used in multilayer perceptrons for modifying weights through error propagation from output layer to input layer. It includes two types of errors: one for output neurons and another for hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig.

glossary.definition: A phase in neural network processing where inputs are propagated from input layer to output layer. This is one of two main phases in the backpropagation algorithm alongside with backpropagation.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where dataset contains data objects together with right output labels. The goal is to predict known outputs when feeding particular data objects into the network.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output in a neural network. It indicates how far off the prediction is from the actual value.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems. It involves incorporating formally described knowledge into intelligent systems to support their performance.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine. The knowledge base maps real-world objects to computational ones while the inference engine uses this knowledge for problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a repository where production rules are stored; its structure and sequence determine how to search it and how quickly it will be searched.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: the processing mechanism of an expert system responsible for applying rules from the knowledge base to derive new facts or conclusions.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: A system that stores unrelated facts such as purchase transactions or student grades without representing the relationships between these facts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A formal convention and rule system used to describe knowledge within an intelligent computer system, allowing for logical or structured representations such as logical statements or directed graphs.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Approaches in AI divided into weak methods, which use general techniques like search or logic without domain-specific knowledge, and strong methods that rely on extensive problem-related knowledge.
glossary.sentence: we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods...and strong methods...

glossary.definition: A specialist skilled in AI methods and tools who works with domain experts to describe and represent knowledge within a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence...

glossary.definition: An individual with deep factual, rule-based, and practical knowledge in a specific problem domain, often collaborating with a knowledge engineer to build an intelligent system.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules)...

glossary.definition: An automated tool used by domain experts or knowledge engineers to describe, analyze, and build structured representations of knowledge in a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly...

glossary.definition: An intelligent, knowledge-based computer system designed to solve complex problems in a specialized domain using methods and performance levels similar to those of human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems.

glossary.definition: A category of knowledge representation schema where knowledge is expressed through logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, and logical operators.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements...

glossary.definition: A type of schema that represents knowledge as a directed labelled graph, where nodes are objects/concepts and arcs show relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph...

glossary.definition: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge...

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: A category of schema that represents knowledge in the form of procedures or instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Psychological theories suggesting that human concepts are connected through associations, influencing how meaning is derived from related ideas.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind...

glossary.definition: A structure in which concepts are organized in levels, with general features at higher levels and more specific ones lower down, influencing knowledge retrieval times.
glossary.sentence: Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner.

glossary.definition: The average time taken for individuals to respond to questions, used as an indicator of how knowledge is stored and retrieved from memory.
glossary.sentence: They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence.

glossary.definition: An experiment conducted by Collins and Quillian in 1969 that demonstrated how people organize knowledge hierarchically, influencing reaction times when recalling information about birds.
glossary.sentence: In 1969, Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge.

glossary.definition: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Semantic networks have a number of advantages...

glossary.definition: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a”...

glossary.definition: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of "is an instance/example of"...

glossary.definition: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of "Part of"...

glossary.definition: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of "property/has a property of"...

glossary.definition: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values. This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of "value"...

glossary.definition: Linguistic relations allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations...

glossary.definition: A feature in frame-based expert systems where sub-class and instance frames inherit slots and their values from class frames.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.

glossary.definition: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975.

glossary.definition: A structured way of storing knowledge about each object in one place, providing hierarchical organization, allowing representation of various knowledge, supporting inheritance, and allowing adding executable demons to slots.
glossary.sentence: Frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: An expert system that uses frames to capture and represent knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Frames are used to capture and represent knowledge in a frame-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” . Thus, to create a knowledge base for the frame-based expert system, we need to define and interrelate frames.

glossary.definition: A component within a frame that holds specific information about an object. Slot values can trigger demons when accessed, changed, added, or removed.
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed... IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified... IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified... and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted. The frame Card#2967 contains the calculated value for the slot Ensured services “Luggage insurance for free ” ;

glossary.definition: The specific values assigned to properties within slots in a frame structure.
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame. Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames representing extended knowledge about slot values.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: A frame containing common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: There are two main types of frames (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind (Figure 4). Traditionally slot values in the class frame are empty, or they contain default values. Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5). The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.

glossary.definition: A frame representing detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames. However, instance frames can also contain slots that are not defined for class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: A type of frame relationship relating a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.
glossary.sentence: Three types of relations are used to relate frames ( Negnevitsky , 2011): Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: A type of frame relationship allowing representation of extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.definition: A type of frame relationship allowing definition of any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: Regular procedures or demons added to slots within a frame structure.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code added to slots within a frame structure.
glossary.sentence: In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: Procedural elements in frame-based expert systems that implement actions.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: Frames acquired when demons are assigned to frames.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem, presented in the form IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion. It can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by logic operators AND, OR or both.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A production rule is presented in the following way: IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion... a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both.

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system consisting of three parts: a knowledge base containing production rules, working memory, and an inference engine. The knowledge base includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts: a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine. The knowledge base typically includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge: knowledge units in the THEN part of one rule make the IF part of another rule (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired when the THEN part of rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine used when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine used when a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: rules used by human experts that have a specific structure and order which must be preserved in building a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving. Therefore, the structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: a method for choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during a conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution method where more specific rules are fired because they process more information than general ones.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: expert systems that use production rules as a natural way of representing and organizing problem-solving knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ).

glossary.definition: a component in an expert system where data is stored and accessed, with time tags attached to each fact for conflict resolution purposes.
glossary.sentence: the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).
